label: Logit Lens
definition: An interpretability technique that reads a transformer's intermediate predictions by projecting hidden states from each layer through the model's output head, revealing how its guess about the next token forms and changes across depth.

body.text: The logit lens is a technique for watching a transformer make up its mind. A language model produces its final prediction by taking the hidden state at the last layer and multiplying it through an unembedding matrix to get logits over the vocabulary. The logit lens applies that same final projection not just at the end but at every intermediate layer, decoding each layer's hidden state as if it were the model's answer right then. The result is a layer-by-layer trace of the model's evolving best guess for the next token, a way to see the prediction crystallize as information flows upward through the network.

This matters because it opens a window onto computation that is otherwise invisible. Reading the intermediate logits often shows the correct answer appearing well before the final layer, sometimes early and then being refined, suppressed, or overridden, which tells you roughly where in the network a given decision is made. That makes the logit lens a fast, almost free first probe of a model's internal dynamics: with nothing more than the model's own output head, you get a coarse map of which layers do which work, useful for localizing the behavior you later study with full circuit analysis.

The technique rests on a structural assumption about transformers: the residual stream is a shared space that every layer reads from and writes to, and the final unembedding is a linear readout of that space. If the meaning of the residual stream is reasonably consistent across depth, then applying the output head early is a sensible approximation of "what does the model currently believe." That assumption is imperfect, because earlier layers do not write in exactly the same basis the final layer expects, which can make the raw logit lens noisy or misleading in some models. The tuned lens addresses this by fitting a small learned transform per layer so that each hidden state is mapped into the output basis before decoding, yielding a more faithful and reliable trace at the cost of the simplicity that made the original so appealing.

The logit lens sits alongside the probing classifier as a way to read what a model represents, but with a distinctive angle: instead of training a fresh classifier for some external property, it borrows the model's own vocabulary projection, so it speaks in tokens the model already knows. It pairs naturally with circuit work, including the study of the induction head, where it can show a copied or pattern-completed token emerging in the middle layers. As a quick lens onto the intermediate state of the residual stream, it remains one of the most accessible entry points into the internal life of a transformer.
